member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration
member.summary:
Root configuration document for a single client. Defines everything the system needs to know about a client: which services it may call, how fast it may call them, and how many resource-pool slots it may hold.
Settings hierarchy and override model
Several settings follow a client-level default → per-target override pattern. The client-level value acts as the fallback; a per-target value (when non-null) wins.
is the client-wide default. overrides it for one specific service.
is the client-wide default. overrides it for one specific service.
applies across all services as a blanket throttle. adds a separate, per-service throttle that is evaluated in addition to (not instead of) the global one.
Access model
Access is deny-by-default. A client can only reach a service if: (1) is true, (2) a entry exists in for that service, and (3) is true. After those static checks pass, the request still has to clear global and per-client rate limits before it is granted.
Resource pool quotas
Entries in set per-client concurrency caps on resource pools. They do not grant or deny access on their own - they only limit how many slots this specific client may hold at the same time. If no entry exists for a pool, the client can still acquire slots (up to the pool's system-wide ), but has no individual cap.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.Id
member.summary: Unique identifier for the client.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.Name
member.summary: Human-readable display name.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.IsEnabled
member.summary: Whether this client is currently active. Disabled clients are rejected immediately with a 403 Forbidden response - no further checks (rate limits, service access, quotas) are evaluated.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.ContributesToGlobalLimits
member.summary:
Whether this client's requests count toward the shared global rate-limit counters ( entities with or ).
When true (the default), every granted or evaluated request from this client increments the global counter, consuming capacity for all other clients too. Set to false for internal/trusted clients whose traffic should not eat into the shared budget.
Can be overridden per service via .

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.ExemptFromGlobalLimits
member.summary:
Whether this client is exempt from being denied by global rate limits.
This is independent of . A client can contribute to the counter (so its traffic is visible) yet still be exempt from denial (so it is never blocked). Conversely, a client can be non-contributing but still subject to global denials.
Defaults to false. Can be overridden per service via .

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.GlobalRateLimit
member.summary:
Optional per-client rate limit applied across all service requests from this client, regardless of which service is being called.
Useful for throttling noisy or spammy clients without disabling them entirely. When a service also has its own , both limits are evaluated - the most restrictive one wins.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.Services
member.summary:
Per-service access settings, keyed by service ID.
If a service ID is absent from this dictionary, the client has no relationship with that service and requests are rejected with (401).

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.ResourcePools
member.summary:
Per-resource-pool quota settings, keyed by resource pool ID.
Each entry caps how many concurrent slots this client may hold in the corresponding pool. Pools not listed here have no per-client cap (the resource pool specific still applies).

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientConfiguration.CreatedAt
member.summary: UTC timestamp when this configuration was created.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientRateLimit
member.summary:
Rate-limit configuration for a single client. Reused in two scopes:
Client-wide via : caps the client's total request rate across all services.
Per-service via : caps the client's request rate to one specific service. When both are present, both are evaluated - the most restrictive one wins.
This record only governs per-client throttling. System-wide aggregate limits across all clients are defined separately via .

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientRateLimit.Strategy
member.summary: The rate limiting algorithm to use.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientRateLimit.MaxRequests
member.summary: Maximum requests in the window, or bucket capacity for .

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientRateLimit.Window
member.summary: Window duration, or refill interval for .

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ClientRateLimit.TokensPerRefill
member.summary: Tokens added per refill. Only used with .

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.GlobalRateLimit
member.summary:
A system-wide rate limit shared across all clients for a single target (service or resource pool).
How it differs from
A throttles one individual client. A throttles the aggregate traffic of every client hitting the same target. Both can exist simultaneously - per-client limits prevent any single client from being too noisy, while the global limit protects the target from being overwhelmed even if every client is individually well-behaved.
Contribute vs. exempt
Not every client interacts with the global counter in the same way. Two independent flags control a client's relationship to this limit:
Contributes ( / ): when true, the client's requests increment this counter, consuming shared capacity. Disable this for internal or trusted clients whose traffic should not eat into the budget visible to others.
Exempt ( / ): when true, the client is never denied by this counter, even if it is exhausted. Use this for high-priority clients that must always get through.
These two flags are orthogonal: a client can contribute without being exempt (the common case), be exempt without contributing (high-priority, invisible), both, or neither.
Applicability by target type
For : caps aggregate request throughput (e.g. "this API can handle 10,000 requests/minute total"). For : caps aggregate slot-acquisition frequency, preventing thundering-herd bursts against the pool, independent of the pool's slot capacity.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.GlobalRateLimit.Id
member.summary: Unique identifier for this global rate limit.

member.summary: ID of the service or resource pool this limit applies to.

member.summary: Whether the target is a service or a resource pool. Determines the access-control path that evaluates this limit.

member.summary: The rate-limiting algorithm to use.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourceAllocation
member.summary:
Represents one active (or formerly active) slot held by a client in a resource pool.
Created when a resource aquisition attempt succeeds. Freed in one of two ways: the client explicitly releases the resource (and emits a event), or the allocation outlives its and is cleaned up by a cleanup service (no Released event emitted - the slot is silently reclaimed).

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourceAllocation.Id
member.summary: Unique identifier for this allocation.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourceAllocation.ResourcePoolId
member.summary: ID of the resource pool this slot belongs to.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourceAllocation.ClientId
member.summary: ID of the client holding this slot.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourceAllocation.AcquiredAt
member.summary: UTC timestamp when this slot was acquired.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourceAllocation.ExpiresAt
member.summary: UTC timestamp when this allocation expires if not released.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourceAllocation.IsReleased
member.summary: Whether this allocation has been explicitly released.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePool
member.summary:
Defines a named pool of finite, stateful resources that clients acquire and release (databases, message brokers, streaming endpoints, etc.).
Capacity model
is the hard, system-wide ceiling - no more than this many connections may be active at the same time across all clients. Individual clients can be further restricted via in their dictionary (e.g. "this pool allows 100 total connections, but client X may hold at most 10").
Slot lifecycle
A slot is created on a successful acquire, held by the client for the duration of its work, and freed either by an explicit release or automatically after expires. Expired allocations are cleaned up by a background service (AllocationCleanupService) and do not produce a usage event.
Rate limits on top of slot quotas
Optionally, a with can be configured to cap the frequency of acquisition attempts, independent of how many slots are free. This prevents thundering-herd bursts from overwhelming the backing resource.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePool.Id
member.summary: Unique identifier for the resource pool.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePool.Name
member.summary: Human-readable display name, used for error messages and metrics tags.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePool.MaxSlots
member.summary: Maximum number of concurrent slots available system-wide, across all clients. This is the absolute hard cap - once reached, all further acquisitions are denied until a slot is released or expires.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePool.AllocationTtl
member.summary: Duration after which an unreleased allocation automatically expires. Acts as a safety net for clients that crash or forget to release - without this, leaked slots would permanently reduce the pool's usable capacity.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePool.IsEnabled
member.summary: Whether this resource pool is currently active. Disabled pools reject all acquisition attempts with a response.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePool.CreatedAt
member.summary: UTC timestamp when this resource pool was registered in the system.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePoolSettings
member.summary:
Per-client quota for a resource pool, stored in keyed by pool ID.
This caps how many slots one specific client can hold at the same time, as opposed to which caps the pool system-wide. For example, a pool might allow 100 total connections but limit any single client to 10 so that one greedy consumer cannot starve the others.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ResourcePoolSettings.MaxSlots
member.summary: Maximum number of concurrent slots this client may hold in the pool. Must be ≤ the pool's ; otherwise the system-wide cap would always be hit first.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.Service
member.summary:
Defines a stateless, request/response target that clients can be granted access to (an API, microservice, or web endpoint).
Access to a service is controlled per-client through . Rate limits can be applied per-client (), per-client-per-service (), and globally ( with ).

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.Service.Id
member.summary: Unique identifier for the service.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.Service.Name
member.summary: Human-readable display name, used for error messages and metrics tags.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.Service.IsEnabled
member.summary: Whether this service is currently active. Disabled services reject all requests with a response.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.Service.CreatedAt
member.summary: UTC timestamp when this service was created (or at least, when it was registered in the system).

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ServiceAccessSettings
member.summary:
Per-service access settings for a single client, nested inside (keyed by service ID).
This record controls both the static access decision (is the client allowed at all?) and the dynamic override knobs for rate-limit behavior on this specific service. Any nullable property left as null inherits the client-wide default from .

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ServiceAccessSettings.IsAllowed
member.summary:
Whether the client is allowed to access this service.
If no entry exists at all for this client–service pair, the access check returns (no relationship configured). If an entry exists but is false, the check returns (explicitly blocked).

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ServiceAccessSettings.ContributesToGlobalLimit
member.summary:
Whether this client's requests to this specific service count toward the service's counter.
When null, inherits from . Set explicitly to decouple this service from the client-wide default - for example, to exclude a health-check service from contributing while the client normally contributes everywhere else.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ServiceAccessSettings.ExemptFromGlobalLimit
member.summary:
Whether this client is exempt from being denied by the service's .
When null, inherits from . Set explicitly to grant priority access to a critical service without changing the client's global exemption posture.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.ServiceAccessSettings.RateLimit
member.summary:
Optional per-client rate limit scoped to this service only.
When set, this limit is evaluated in addition to the client's - both must have remaining capacity for the request to be granted. This allows fine-grained throttling (e.g. a client may be allowed 1,000 req/min globally but only 100 req/min to an expensive reporting service).

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageBucket
member.summary: A single time-window of aggregated usage counts within a . The window's duration is implied by the parent snapshot's .

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageBucket.Timestamp
member.summary:
UTC start of this time bucket.
The bucket covers the half-open interval [Timestamp, Timestamp + granularity). For example, with and a timestamp of 2024-01-01T14:00:00Z, the bucket covers 14:00:00–14:59:59.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageBucket.GrantedCount
member.summary: Number of events in this window - successful requests (for services) or successful slot acquisitions (for resource pools).

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageBucket.DeniedCount
member.summary: Total denied events in this window (sum of category counters).

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageBucket.ReleasedCount
member.summary: Number of events in this window. Always 0 for targets because services are stateless and have nothing to release.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageBucket.ActiveCount
member.summary:
Point-in-time snapshot of how many resource-pool slots the client held when this bucket was persisted. Always 0 for targets.
Unlike the other counters (which are cumulative over the bucket's window), this is a gauge captured at flush time. It's useful for observing concurrency trends (e.g. "client X consistently holds 8 of its 10 allowed slots").

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot
member.summary:
A time-series document recording usage for one client against one target at a given .
How snapshots are built
As events are emitted during access checks and resource allocations, they accumulate in an in-memory buffer. A background service periodically drains that buffer, groups the counts by (client, target, granularity), and upserts them into the matching snapshot document. Each flush appends or merges into the list.
Time segments
Snapshots are split into bounded time segments so that no single document grows indefinitely. Each segment covers a fixed time window whose size depends on the granularity (e.g. 1 hour for second-granularity, 1 day for five-minute, 1 week for hour, 1 month for day). The property records which window this document belongs to, and the includes a yyyyMMddHH suffix that makes each segment a separate document. This design means flush only reads and writes the current small segment, prune can drop whole segment documents instead of filtering individual buckets, and retention can grow without any single document becoming too large to handle efficiently.
Composite key
is deterministic: "{ClientId}:{TargetType}:{TargetId}:{Granularity}:{SegmentStart:yyyyMMddHH}". Legacy pre-segmentation documents use the shorter form without the time suffix and have a null ; they expire naturally via retention.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot.Id
member.summary:
A unique identifier for this usage snapshot document.
Segmented format: "{ClientId}:{TargetType}:{TargetId}:{Granularity}:{SegmentStart:yyyyMMddHH}". Legacy format (pre-segmentation): "{ClientId}:{TargetType}:{TargetId}:{Granularity}".

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot.ClientId
member.summary: ID of the client this usage data belongs to.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot.TargetId
member.summary: ID of the service or resource pool being tracked.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot.TargetType
member.summary: The type of target this usage data is tracking.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot.Granularity
member.summary: The "exactness" of data in this snapshot - how aggregated are the time buckets in the snapshot.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot.SegmentStart
member.summary:
The start of the time segment this document covers.
Each segment spans a fixed window determined by the : → 1 hour, → 1 day, → 1 week, → 1 month. All in this document fall within [SegmentStart, SegmentStart + window).
null for legacy pre-segmentation documents that will age out naturally via the retention pruning cycle.

member.name: P:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Entities.UsageSnapshot.Buckets
member.summary: Ordered list of usage aggregations over time, oldest first.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.RateLimitStrategy
member.summary: Defines the algorithm used to enforce rate limits.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.RateLimitStrategy.FixedWindow
member.summary:
Counts requests in fixed, non-overlapping time windows.
Requests are grouped into discrete intervals (e.g. 1 minute), and the count resets at the end of each interval. This is straightforward to implement and efficient but can lead to uneven request distribution, especially if many requests arrive near the end of a window.
For example, with a 1-minute window, all requests from 12:00:00 to 12:00:59 are counted together, and the count resets at 12:01:00. This is simple and efficient but can lead to burstiness at window boundaries.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.RateLimitStrategy.ApproximateSlidingWindow
member.summary:
Approximate sliding window using a weighted average of two adjacent fixed windows.
Unlike a true sliding log (which stores every request timestamp), this approach maintains only two counters and blends the previous window's count with the current window's count proportionally to how far into the current window the request falls. This is memory-efficient and widely used (e.g. Redis, Cloudflare) but may slightly over- or under-count near window boundaries.
For example, with a 1-minute window and a request at 12:00:30, the count would be: (0.5 * count from 11:59:00-11:59:59) + (0.5 * count from 12:00:00-12:00:29)

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.RateLimitStrategy.TokenBucket
member.summary:
Uses a token bucket that refills at a configured rate, allowing controlled bursts.
Each request consumes a token, and tokens are added to the bucket at a steady rate. If the bucket is empty, requests are denied until tokens are replenished. This allows for more flexible rate limiting, accommodating bursts while enforcing an average rate over time.
For example, with a bucket capacity of 10 tokens and a refill rate of 1 token per second, the bucket starts full with 10 tokens. If 10 requests arrive at once, they consume all tokens and are allowed. If an 11th request arrives immediately after, it is denied until at least one token is refilled (after 1 second).

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.PersistenceProvider
member.summary:
Identifies the storage backend used for persistence.
In this context, persistence refers to the ability of multiple instances of this application to act in unison, sharing the same data and state across a distributed environment. The choice of persistence provider determines how data is stored & accessed across instances.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.PersistenceProvider.JsonFile
member.summary: File-based JSON storage, intended for local development, or for a shared persistent volume.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.PersistenceProvider.MongoDb
member.summary: A MongoDB document database, intended for production use in a distributed environment.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.PersistenceProvider.Redis
member.summary: A Redis in-memory data store, intended for production use where performance is critical and compromises on durability are acceptable.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.PersistenceProvider.Lucene
member.summary: A Lucene.NET embedded search index, intended for PVC-based deployments that need full-text and field-level search without an external database.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.PersistenceProvider.Sqlite
member.summary: SQLite storage for usage statistics (local or single-instance deployments).

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.StorageRole
member.summary: Identifies a logical storage domain. Each role can be independently mapped to a different , allowing mixed-backend deployments (e.g. Redis for rate-limit state, MongoDB for statistics).

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.StorageRole.RateLimiting

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.StorageRole.Allocations

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.StorageRole.Statistics

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.BucketGranularity
member.summary:
Controls the width of time windows used to aggregate s within a .
Finer granularities (e.g. ) give precise, near-real-time visibility but produce many buckets and are therefore retained for a shorter period. Coarser granularities (e.g. ) compress data and are kept longer for trend analysis. The system maintains multiple snapshots per (client, target) combination - one for each granularity - so that dashboards can zoom from seconds to days without re-aggregating.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.BucketGranularity.Second
member.summary: 1-second buckets. Highest resolution, shortest retention.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.BucketGranularity.OneMinute
member.summary: 1-minute buckets. Bridges second-level live data and five-minute rollups.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.BucketGranularity.FiveMinute
member.summary: 5-minute buckets. Balances precision with storage cost; suitable for short-term operational dashboards.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.BucketGranularity.Hour
member.summary: 1-hour buckets. Lower resolution, longer retention; suitable for medium-term capacity planning.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.BucketGranularity.Day
member.summary: 1-day buckets. Coarsest resolution, longest retention; suitable for long-term trend analysis.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.TargetType
member.summary:
Distinguishes the two fundamental kinds of targets the system manages access to.
The target type drives which access-control path is taken and which constraints apply. targets go through AccessControlService and are governed purely by rate limits (request frequency). targets go through ResourceAllocationService and are governed by both slot quotas (concurrency) and rate limits (acquisition frequency).
This enum is used throughout the system - in to scope a rate-limit rule, in tracking to categorize events, and in usage snapshots to partition statistics.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.TargetType.Service
member.summary:
A stateless, request/response target - typically an API, microservice, or web endpoint.
Clients send discrete requests and receive responses; they do not hold open connections or occupy capacity between requests. The caller does not care which instance handles the request, only that the service is reachable.
Throttled exclusively via rate limits: per-client (), per-client-per-service (), and global-per-service ().

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.TargetType.ResourcePool
member.summary:
A stateful, connection-oriented target - typically a database, message broker, or streaming endpoint.
Clients acquire a slot (connection) and hold it for the duration of their work. The server must maintain state for each active connection, so both the total number of concurrent slots () and each client's share () are capped.
In addition to slot quotas, resource pools can also have a that caps the frequency of acquisition attempts - preventing thundering-herd bursts even when slots are available.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.UsageEventType
member.summary:
Classifies the outcome of an access-control or resource-allocation decision so that usage statistics can be recorded and later aggregated by (Service or ResourcePool).
How the system uses these values
Every time a client interacts with a (via AccessControlService.CheckAccessAsync) or a (via ResourceAllocationService.AcquireAsync / ReleaseAsync), exactly one is emitted to the IUsageRecorder. These events are buffered in memory, then periodically flushed to persistent storage by UsagePersistenceService, where they become the GrantedCount, DeniedCount, and ReleasedCount fields on each usage bucket.
Services vs. Resource Pools - why the distinction matters
targets are stateless request/response endpoints (APIs, microservices). The only throttling mechanism is rate limiting - capping how many requests a client (or all clients globally) can make in a time window. A request is either or ; there is nothing to release.
targets are stateful connection-oriented resources (databases, message brokers, streamers). They are governed by two independent constraints that serve different purposes:
Slot quotas (concurrency caps) - limit how many connections can exist at the same time. sets the system-wide ceiling (e.g. "this database accepts 100 total connections"), while in a client's sets a per-client ceiling (e.g. "this client may hold at most 5 connections"). A slot is occupied from until (or until the allocation's TTL expires and is cleaned up).
Rate limits (acquisition-frequency caps) - limit how often clients can attempt to acquire slots, configured as a with . This exists to prevent thundering-herd scenarios: even when plenty of slots are available, a burst of hundreds of simultaneous acquire requests could overwhelm the backing resource. The rate limit smooths out that burst without reducing the pool's actual capacity.
Because these two constraints are orthogonal, a pool can deny an acquisition for either reason independently: "you already hold too many slots" (quota) or "too many acquire attempts this minute" (rate limit). Both result in a event - the distinction is in the exception and metric tag, not the event type.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.UsageEventType.Granted
member.summary:
The access check passed and the requested action was allowed.
For : The client's request cleared every gate - the client is enabled, the service is enabled, the client has an explicit access entry with IsAllowed = true, the global service rate limit (shared across all clients) has capacity, and the per-client rate limit (service-specific or the client's global rate limit) has capacity. The request is forwarded to the service for processing.
For : A slot was successfully allocated. This means the client's per-client quota () was not exhausted, the global acquisition rate limit for this pool was not exceeded, and the system-wide pool capacity () still had room. The slot is now held by the client until it is either explicitly or it expires after the pool's .

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.UsageEventType.Denied
member.summary:
The access check failed and the requested action was blocked.
For : The request was rejected at one of several stages (checked in this order): the client is disabled, the service is disabled, no access entry exists for this client–service pair (401 Unauthorized), the entry has IsAllowed = false (403 Forbidden), the global service rate limit is exhausted, or the per-client rate limit is exhausted (429 Too Many Requests). The first failing check short-circuits the pipeline.
For : Slot acquisition was rejected for one of three reasons (checked in this order): the client's per-client slot quota for this pool has been reached, the global acquisition rate limit for this pool has been exceeded, or the pool's system-wide slot capacity is full. All three produce a event; the specific cause is captured in the associated metric tag and exception type.

member.name: F:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Enums.UsageEventType.Released
member.summary:
A previously held resource pool slot was explicitly given back by the client.
This event only applies to . When a client calls ResourceAllocationService.ReleaseAsync, the slot is marked as released and becomes immediately available for other clients (or the same client) to acquire.
Allocations that are not explicitly released will eventually expire based on and be cleaned up by the background AllocationCleanupService. Expired allocations do not produce a event - only explicit client-initiated releases do. This means the ratio of to events can reveal how often clients are relying on TTL expiration instead of cleaning up after themselves.
This event is never emitted for because service interactions are stateless request/response exchanges with nothing to release.

member.name: T:ClientManager.Shared.Models.Search.DocumentQuery
member.summary:
Composable query model for searching documents in a collection. Supports field-level filters, sorting, and pagination. Stores translate this to their native query language, or fall back to in-memory evaluation.
Properties use public setters for JSON deserialization from HTTP request bodies. Fluent methods (, , ) are convenience shortcuts for building queries in code.
